questions.passage:
The first time I stood in front of an orchestra I believed, with the serene idiocy of the well-prepared, that I was there to be obeyed. I had studied the score until its margins were black. I had practiced my beat patterns in the mirror until my right arm could have kept time through an earthquake. I raised the baton, gave what I considered a commanding upbeat, and sixty professional musicians came in at three slightly different moments, producing a sound like a wave collapsing on gravel. They were not rebelling. They were answering, quite accurately, the confusion I had shown them.

My teacher, an elderly Hungarian who had survived worse podiums than mine, watched from the back of the hall. His entire comment afterward was: 'The orchestra plays the conductor.' I took this at first for consolation, one of those reversible aphorisms teachers keep in their pockets. It took me two years to understand that he had given me a technical description. The sound an ensemble makes is a rendering, faithful to the point of cruelty, of what the person on the podium has actually communicated, as opposed to what she intended, believed, or wrote in her margins. If the winds drag, the honest first question is not what is wrong with the winds.

The apprenticeship that followed was a program of humiliations, each instructive. I learned that the baton produces no sound and therefore has no power except anticipation: the gesture must live slightly ahead of the music, showing players not where the beat is but where it is about to be. Arrive with the beat and you are merely dancing to your own orchestra. I learned that a conductor's face is louder than her arm, that panic travels from the eyes to the brass in under a second, and that the appearance of calm is not vanity but information.

The hardest lesson concerned my mouth. In rehearsal I talked. I explained context, quoted the composer's letters, described the color I wanted in the violas with metaphors I had polished at home. The players listened politely and played as before. One afternoon, out of exhaustion, I ran a rehearsal almost without speaking: when a passage failed, I stopped, looked at the section, sang two bars the way I heard them, and gave the upbeat again. The difference was immediate and slightly insulting. Years of eloquence had accomplished less than one honestly sung phrase. Musicians, I finally saw, do not convert descriptions into sound; they convert sound and gesture into sound. My metaphors had been for me.

I do not want to dissolve the podium into false modesty. Somebody must choose a tempo; somebody must decide whose line matters in bar ninety; an orchestra without a single governing intention produces committee music, correct and dead. Authority is real. But I had the direction of it wrong. Authority is not something a conductor brings to the ensemble and imposes; it is something the ensemble extends, provisionally, to a person who demonstrably hears more than they do and can show what she hears. It is renewed or withdrawn every few bars, in a continuous silent plebiscite, and no appointment, reputation, or beautiful arm can overrule the vote.

What I do now, on good nights, would have disappointed my younger self. She wanted to command the sea. I listen; I show what I hear a moment before it must happen; I stay out of the way of players who know their business. The orchestra plays the conductor, and after all these years my whole ambition is to be worth playing.
questions.stem: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Verbal explanation has no legitimate place in orchestral rehearsal and should be replaced entirely by gesture.
choices.B: Professional orchestras perform best when conductors abandon interpretive decisions to the players.
choices.C: Conducting technique consists chiefly of beat patterns and baton control that must be thoroughly mastered before a musician can lead a professional ensemble.
choices.D: The narrator came to understand conducting as anticipatory listening whose authority is granted by the ensemble rather than imposed upon it.

explanations.A: The narrator's discovery is that her explanations accomplished less than singing and gesture, but the passage stops short of banning speech; the point is about how musicians convert communication into sound.
explanations.B: The narrator expressly refuses 'false modesty,' insisting that somebody must choose tempos and priorities and that leaderless playing yields 'committee music, correct and dead.'
explanations.C: Mirror-practiced beat patterns belong to the younger self whose confidence the passage mocks; the education recounted is precisely the discovery that such technique was not the substance of the job.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage traces a reversal from expecting to be obeyed to understanding that gesture must live ahead of the music and that authority 'is something the ensemble extends, provisionally,' renewed in a 'continuous silent plebiscite.'

questions.stem: The narrator's attitude toward her younger self is best described as:

choices.A: bitter resentment of the training that misled her.
choices.B: affectionate mockery of a confidence she now sees as misplaced.
choices.C: unqualified admiration for the thoroughness of her preparation.
choices.D: indifference toward a period she considers irrelevant to her present work.

explanations.A: The narrator blames her own assumptions rather than her training, and phrases like 'serene idiocy of the well-prepared' carry amusement, not bitterness.
explanations.B: Correct. She calls her early certainty 'serene idiocy,' notes that her ambitions 'would have disappointed my younger self,' and describes wanting 'to command the sea,' a tone of fond ridicule directed at her own misplaced confidence.
explanations.C: Although she describes genuinely thorough preparation, the passage frames that preparation as beside the point, so the admiration is heavily qualified by irony.
explanations.D: Far from irrelevant, the younger self's errors structure the whole account; each humiliation is presented as instructive for the conductor she became.

questions.stem: As the narrator comes to understand it, the teacher's remark that 'the orchestra plays the conductor' means that:

choices.A: orchestras deliberately expose inexperienced conductors by exaggerating whatever errors appear in their gestures.
choices.B: an ensemble will eventually perform well regardless of who stands on the podium.
choices.C: the sound an ensemble produces is an accurate rendering of what the conductor has actually communicated.
choices.D: conductors must learn each player's part before they can earn the ensemble's respect.

explanations.A: The narrator explicitly denies rebellion or malice; the players 'were answering, quite accurately, the confusion I had shown them,' which is fidelity rather than exaggeration.
explanations.B: The remark assigns the podium more consequence, not less; the whole point is that the conductor's actual communication determines the result.
explanations.C: Correct. The narrator says she eventually recognized the aphorism as 'a technical description': the ensemble's sound is 'a rendering, faithful to the point of cruelty,' of what the conductor communicated rather than what she intended.
explanations.D: Knowing every part appears nowhere in the passage; the lesson concerns the difference between intention and communication, not the breadth of the conductor's study.

questions.stem: It can most reasonably be inferred that the narrator's teacher believed that:

choices.A: conducting cannot be taught and must be discovered through public failure.
choices.B: a conductor's failures are more usefully traced to her own communication than to the players.
choices.C: young conductors should avoid appearing before professional orchestras until their baton technique is fully complete.
choices.D: aphorisms are more effective pedagogy than demonstration.

explanations.A: The teacher watched, taught, and supplied the observation that organized the narrator's entire apprenticeship, which presumes conducting can be taught even if lessons arrive through failure.
explanations.B: Correct. His single comment redirected attention from the players' entrance to the conductor's own signal, and the narrator's gloss makes the principle explicit: 'If the winds drag, the honest first question is not what is wrong with the winds.'
explanations.C: Nothing suggests the teacher opposed the narrator's early podium experience; he attended it and turned it into instruction.
explanations.D: The teacher's aphorism worked only after two years of experience unpacked it, and the passage's decisive breakthroughs come through singing and gesture, so the passage gives no ground for ranking sayings above demonstration.

questions.stem: The paragraph describing the nearly silent rehearsal serves primarily to:

choices.A: show that the narrator's early podium failures stemmed principally from her inadequate and superficial study of the printed orchestral score.
choices.B: argue that exhaustion often produces a conductor's best work.
choices.C: illustrate the players' growing personal loyalty to the narrator.
choices.D: present the turning point at which the narrator learned that musicians respond to sound and gesture rather than to description.

explanations.A: The narrator's study was exhaustive from the start; the silent rehearsal exposes the futility of her explanations, not any gap in her preparation.
explanations.B: Exhaustion is merely the accident that occasioned the experiment; the paragraph draws its conclusion about communication, not about fatigue.
explanations.C: The players' improvement is attributed to a change in the medium of instruction, and the narrator finds it 'slightly insulting,' hardly a portrait of growing personal loyalty.
explanations.D: Correct. The episode delivers the paragraph's stated lesson: one honestly sung phrase outdid years of eloquence because musicians 'convert sound and gesture into sound,' the discovery around which the narrator reorganizes her craft.

questions.stem: Which of the following situations most closely parallels the narrator's discovery about rehearsal?

choices.A: A pottery instructor whose students improve markedly once she stops lecturing about form and instead throws a pot while they watch her hands.
choices.B: A surgeon who reduces operating-room errors by requiring every member of her team to recite a standardized checklist aloud before each operation.
choices.C: A novelist who abandons outlines and finds that improvised drafts are more vivid.
choices.D: A coach who motivates athletes by describing the championships won by earlier teams.

explanations.A: Correct. Like the narrator singing two bars instead of polishing metaphors, the instructor replaces verbal description with a direct demonstration in the medium itself, and the students, like the musicians, convert what they perceive rather than what is described to them.
explanations.B: A recited checklist adds structured speech to the work; the narrator's breakthrough ran in the opposite direction, subtracting talk in favor of demonstration.
explanations.C: The novelist changes her private method of composition; the narrator's lesson concerns how to communicate with other performers, not how to plan her own work.
explanations.D: Motivational storytelling is more talk of exactly the kind the narrator found inert; nothing in the coach's approach substitutes demonstration for description.
